mnemonics.title: Process States Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: N-R-W-T-R: 'Never Run When Tired' — New → Ready → Running → Waiting → Terminated. Remember: A process 'runs' when it gets CPU, 'waits' for I/O, and goes to 'terminated' when finished.

mnemonics.title: Scheduling Criteria
mnemonics.content: CUT-TWR: 'CPU Utilization Tends With Great Response' — CPU Utilization, Throughput, Turnaround time, Waiting time, Response time. These are the 5 key criteria to compare algorithms.

mnemonics.title: SJF vs SRTF
mnemonics.content: SJF = 'Static Job First' (non-preemptive), SRTF = 'Shortest Remaining Time First' (preemptive). Think: SRTF has 'Remaining' — it can take away CPU if a shorter job arrives.

mnemonics.title: Algorithm Properties
mnemonics.content: FCFS: 'First Come, First Served' — FIFO queue; SJF: 'Shortest Job First' — optimal for average waiting time; RR: 'Round Robin' — fair time-slice rotation; Priority: 'Important tasks first'.

mnemonics.title: Context Switching Steps
mnemonics.content: Save → Switch → Load: 1) Save state of current process to PCB, 2) Switch to new process, 3) Load state of new process from its PCB. Like bookmarking one book and opening another.

acronyms.usage: Data structure that stores all information about a process (registers, program counter, memory limits, I/O status). Every process has its own PCB — think of it as the process's 'identity card'.
Time period a process executes on CPU before needing I/O. Short bursts = I/O-bound; long bursts = CPU-bound. Scheduling decisions are made at burst boundaries.
Non-preemptive algorithm. Processes served in order of arrival. Simple but can cause 'convoy effect' (long process blocks short ones).
Non-preemptive. Picks process with shortest next CPU burst. Gives minimum average waiting time but requires knowing burst lengths in advance (difficult in practice).
Preemptive version of SJF. If a new process arrives with shorter burst than remaining time of current process, CPU is taken away. Also called SRTF.
Preemptive. Each process gets fixed time quantum (slice). Good for time-sharing. Performance depends on quantum size — too small = overhead, too large = FCFS.
Multiple queues with different priorities. Processes move between queues based on CPU burst behavior. Adapts to process type (CPU-bound vs I/O-bound).
Module that gives CPU control to process selected by short-term scheduler. Involves context switch, switching to user mode, jumping to correct location in program.

visualTips.visualization: Draw a circle with 5 boxes: New → Ready → Running → Waiting → Terminated. Arrows show valid transitions. Ready and Waiting are 'holding areas'; Running is the only active box.
Imagine a ticket counter line. First person in line gets served first. No one can cut. The whole line waits if the first person takes long (convoy effect).
Imagine tasks as people with different job lengths. Shortest job goes first — like prioritizing quick tasks to reduce overall wait time. Draw them sorted by length.
Think of a merry-go-round (carousel). Each child (process) gets 1 ride (time quantum), then must wait for all others before riding again. Fair but can be slow.
Think of two people reading the same book. Person A bookmarks page 50, closes book; Person B opens book, bookmarks their page, starts reading from page 10. The bookmark = PCB.

analogies.analogy: A process is like a program in motion — think of a restaurant order ticket (the recipe) vs the chef cooking it (execution). The order ticket is the program; the cooking process is the process.
PCB is like a student's report card or a soldier's personal file — it contains all important info (grades/records) stored separately from the person (process) itself.
CPU scheduler is like a teacher distributing a single computer among students — decides which student gets computer time, for how long, based on some fairness rule.
FCFS is like a bus stop — first passenger to arrive gets on first. If a slow passenger boards, everyone behind waits longer.
SJF is like a hospital emergency — critical but quick cases treated before long surgeries. Prioritizes shortest tasks to reduce overall patient wait time.
Round Robin is like a group study rotation — each student gets 5 minutes to speak, then passes turn. Everyone gets equal time regardless of task length.
Context switching is like switching between two apps on your phone — the phone saves where you were in App A (save state), then loads App B (load state). There's a slight delay.
Throughput is like a factory assembly line — how many cars roll out per hour. Higher throughput = more processes completed per unit time.
